Lens III — Layer Stratigraphy

§§3.1.3a–c — Layer-by-layer analysis of 6 embedding models (12–24 layers each). Modern text-embedding models transform each input term through a series of successive computational stages called layers; at each layer the model refines its internal numerical representation of the term, and only the final layer’s output is normally used as the term’s embedding. This lens opens the black box: instead of examining only the final output, it extracts the representation at every intermediate layer and measures how it changes. Metrics: cosine drift between consecutive layers (how much a term’s vector moves at each step), k-nearest-neighbor Jaccard instability (whether a term’s closest associates change identity from one layer to the next), domain signal (rank-biserial r: do terms from the same branch of law cluster together at each depth?), RSA convergence (Spearman ρ vs. final layer: how early does the overall distance pattern stabilise?), and Neighborhood Trajectory Analysis (NTA) for selected terms (tracking the exact identity of each term’s nearest neighbors across layers).

Domain signal and RSA convergence per layer. Embedding models process text through a series of successive computational stages called "layers" — typically between 12 and 24, depending on the model. Each layer refines the model’s internal numerical representation of a term, and only the very last layer’s output is normally used as the term’s final embedding. This chart tracks two distinct quantities across all layers of each model (use the dropdown in the upper-right corner to switch between models).

The solid line (left axis) shows the domain signal at each layer: it answers the question "at this depth in the model, do terms belonging to the same branch of law (e.g., two criminal-law terms) tend to have more similar representations than terms from different branches?" The metric used is the rank-biserial $r$ from a Mann-Whitney $U$ test. A value of $r > 0$ means that same-domain pairs are, on average, closer together than cross-domain pairs; $r = 0$ means no detectable separation; $r < 0$ would mean same-domain pairs are actually farther apart.

The dashed line (right axis) shows the RSA convergence: it answers the question "how similar is the full pattern of pairwise distances at layer $\ell$ to the pattern at the final layer?" This is measured by the Spearman rank correlation $\rho$ between the two distance matrices. A value of $\rho = 1$ means the rank ordering of all 78,606 pairwise distances is identical to the final layer’s ordering; lower values mean the distance pattern at that layer is still different from the final output.

Definitions

Domain signal $r_\ell$ (solid line, left axis): at each layer $\ell$, the model’s internal representations of all 397 core legal terms are extracted. From these representations, a 397×397 distance matrix is computed: each cell records how different two terms’ vectors are at that layer. This matrix is then split into two groups of pairs: intra-domain pairs (two terms from the same branch of law, e.g., two criminal-law terms) and inter-domain pairs (two terms from different branches). The Mann-Whitney rank-biserial $r$ measures the degree of separation between these two groups:

$$r_\ell = 1 - \frac{2\,U_\ell}{n_{\text{intra}} \cdot n_{\text{inter}}}$$

In plain language: $r > 0$ means that intra-domain distances tend to be smaller than inter-domain distances — terms from the same branch of law are, on average, closer together in the model’s representational space. $r = 0$ means there is no detectable difference between same-domain and cross-domain distances. $r < 0$ would mean that same-domain terms are actually farther apart than cross-domain terms.

RSA convergence $\rho_\ell$

(dashed line, right axis): the Spearman rank correlation between the distance matrix at layer $\ell$ and the distance matrix at the final layer $L$:

$$\rho_\ell = \text{Spearman}\!\left(\text{upper\_tri}(\text{RDM}_\ell),\; \text{upper\_tri}(\text{RDM}_L)\right)$$

In plain language: Spearman’s $\rho$ compares the rank ordering of all 78,606 pairwise distances at layer $\ell$ with the rank ordering at the final layer. A value of $\rho = 1$ means the two rank orderings are identical — every pair of terms is in the exact same relative position as in the final output. Lower values mean the distance pattern at that layer has not yet converged to its final form. At the final layer itself, $\rho_L = 1$ by definition (any matrix is perfectly correlated with itself).

Cosine drift per domain and layer transition. Between any two consecutive layers in an embedding model, each term’s internal numerical representation (called a "hidden state" or "vector") changes. Cosine drift quantifies how large that change is: it measures the angular difference between the vector at layer $\ell$ and the vector at layer $\ell+1$. A drift value of 0 means the representation did not change at all; values approaching 1 mean a large change (the two vectors point in very different directions); values above 1 are theoretically possible but rare in practice.

Each curve in this chart shows the average drift across all terms belonging to one branch of law (e.g., all criminal-law terms, all constitutional-law terms). This allows comparison of whether certain legal domains undergo more representational change at particular layers. The x-axis spans all layer transitions: for a 24-layer model, these are layer 0→1, 1→2, 2→3, …, 23→24. The y-axis shows the mean drift value. Use the dropdown to switch between models.

Definition

For each term $t$ and layer transition $\ell \to \ell+1$:

$$\text{drift}(t, \ell) = 1 - \cos\!\left(\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell)},\; \mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell+1)}\right)$$

Here, $\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell)}$ is the model’s internal representation (a high-dimensional numerical vector) of term $t$ at layer $\ell$. The cosine of the angle between two vectors measures their directional similarity: a cosine of 1 means the two vectors point in exactly the same direction (identical representation), a cosine of 0 means they are perpendicular (unrelated directions), and a cosine of −1 means they point in opposite directions. Drift is defined as 1 minus this cosine, so it ranges from 0 (no change at all between layers) to 2 (maximally different directions), though values above 1 are rare in practice. In short: drift quantifies how much a term’s numerical representation moves when the model transitions from one computational stage to the next.

Each curve is the mean drift across all terms belonging to one branch of law. The colour legend identifies the branch.

Neighborhood instability per domain and layer transition. For a given term at a given layer, the $k$-nearest neighbors (here $k = 7$) are the 7 other terms whose numerical representations (vectors) are most similar to it at that layer. For example, at a certain layer the 7 nearest neighbors of "negligence" might be {manslaughter, liability, damages, recklessness, tort, duty, fault}. The Jaccard distance compares two such neighbor sets across consecutive layers: it counts how many neighbors are shared and divides by the total number of distinct neighbors appearing in either set. Concretely: if 5 of the 7 neighbors remain the same between layer $\ell$ and layer $\ell+1$, the intersection has 5 terms and the union has 9 terms (7 + 7 − 5), so the overlap is 5/9, and the Jaccard distance is $J = 1 - 5/9 \approx 0.44$. A Jaccard distance of $J = 0$ means the neighbor set is completely identical across the transition; $J = 1$ means the two sets are entirely disjoint (no shared neighbors at all).

Each curve shows the mean Jaccard distance across all terms in one branch of law. A high value at a particular transition means that, on average, the identity of each term’s closest associates is changing rapidly at that layer — the "neighborhood" is being reorganised.

Definition

For each term $t$ and layer transition $\ell \to \ell+1$, the $k$-nearest-neighbor sets ($k=7$) are compared:

$$J(t, \ell) = 1 - \frac{|\text{kNN}(t, \ell) \cap \text{kNN}(t, \ell+1)|}{|\text{kNN}(t, \ell) \cup \text{kNN}(t, \ell+1)|}$$

A concrete example: suppose that at layer 5, the 7 nearest neighbors of "negligence" are {manslaughter, liability, damages, recklessness, tort, duty, fault}. At layer 6, they become {liability, damages, tort, duty, contributory, breach, carelessness}. The intersection (terms present in both sets) has 4 terms: liability, damages, tort, duty. The union (all distinct terms appearing in either set) has 10 terms. Therefore the Jaccard similarity is 4/10 = 0.4, and the Jaccard distance is $J = 1 - 4/10 = 0.6$.

Each curve is the mean $J$ across all terms in one branch of law. Higher values at a particular transition mean that, on average, the identity of each term’s closest associates is changing more rapidly at that layer.

Per-term drift heatmap. This heatmap shows the cosine drift for every individual term at every layer transition, laid out as a two-dimensional colour-coded grid. Each row is one of the 397 core legal terms; each column is one layer transition (e.g., layer 0→1, 1→2, etc.). The colour intensity of each cell encodes the magnitude of the drift at that transition: darker or warmer colours mean larger drift (more change), lighter colours mean smaller drift (less change).

The terms are sorted top-to-bottom by decreasing total drift $\sum_\ell \text{{drift}}(t, \ell)$: terms at the top of the heatmap undergo the most total representational change across all layers, while terms at the bottom are the most stable. Hover over any cell to see the exact term name and drift value. Use the dropdown to compare across models.

Reading the heatmap

Each row is one of 397 core legal terms. Each column is one layer transition $\ell \to \ell+1$ (e.g., layer 0→1, 1→2, etc.). The colour of each cell encodes the cosine drift $\text{drift}(t, \ell)$ at that transition for that term: warmer, more intense colours correspond to larger drift values (more representational change), while lighter colours correspond to smaller drift values (less change).

Terms are sorted top-to-bottom by decreasing total drift $\sum_\ell \text{drift}(t, \ell)$, which is the sum of all per-transition drift values across the entire depth of the model. Terms at the top of the heatmap are those whose representations undergo the most total change as the model processes them through all layers; terms at the bottom are the most stable. Use the model dropdown to compare across models. Hover over any cell to see the exact term name and drift value.

§3.1.3c — Neighborhood Trajectory Analysis (NTA). While the previous charts show aggregate statistics (averages across many terms), this table zooms in on a small number of individually selected terms — chosen because they are polysemous (they carry different meanings in different branches of law), because they have particular cross-tradition relevance, or because they exhibited unusual behaviour in the aggregate metrics. For each selected term, the table lists the exact identity of its $k = 7$ nearest neighbors at several sampled layers (not every layer is shown, only a representative subset). The neighbor pool from which these 7 closest terms are drawn consists of 397 core legal terms (covering 7 branches of law) and 100 non-legal control words from the Swadesh-100 list (basic human-experience terms like "water", "fire", "mother", used as a baseline). As the model processes through its layers, the composition of each term’s neighborhood may change: some neighbors enter the top-7 set, others exit. By reading the table from top to bottom (earlier layers to later layers), one can observe whether the term’s closest associates shift from one branch of law to another, or from non-legal control terms to legal terms, or vice versa.

Legend — How to read the NTA tables

Each table shows one selected term. The rows represent sampled layers (from earlier layers at the top to later layers at the bottom). The columns show the 7 nearest neighbors at that layer, ranked from closest (left, #1) to seventh-closest (right, #7). Each neighbor cell displays the neighbor’s name, its branch of law (domain), and the cosine similarity score (a number between 0 and 1 measuring how close the two vectors are; higher means more similar).

Green cell with green border = a neighbor that entered the top-7 set at this layer. This term was not among the 7 nearest neighbors at the previous sampled layer but has now become close enough to appear. Its arrival may reflect the model reorganising its representation of the focal term at this depth.

Gray italic text ctrl = a control term (a non-legal word from the Swadesh-100 basic vocabulary list, such as "water", "fire", or "night"). Control terms serve as a baseline: if a legal term’s nearest neighbors include many control words, it means the model has not yet developed a specifically legal representation of that term at that layer.

↗ exited = a neighbor that left the top-7 set between sampled layers. This term was among the 7 nearest neighbors at the previous sampled layer but has now moved farther away. Exited terms appear in an orange row beneath the main row, listing all terms that dropped out.

The colored dot ● next to each neighbor’s name marks the branch of law (domain) that neighbor belongs to. Each domain has a consistent colour throughout all tables (e.g., criminal law is always the same colour). This makes it easy to scan visually: a row dominated by dots of one colour means most neighbors at that layer come from the same branch of law.

Domain composition (shown below each table): for each sampled layer, this summary counts how many of the 7 nearest neighbors belong to each branch of law, and how many are legal terms versus control terms. The notation [legal=N, ctrl=M] indicates that N of the 7 neighbors are core legal terms and M are Swadesh-100 control words (N + M = 7). This allows tracking whether a term’s neighborhood shifts from mixed legal/non-legal at early layers to predominantly legal at later layers, or whether certain branches of law gain or lose representation across layers.
